comments.text: This problem has been reported to be asked in: P. Erdos, Problems and results in graph theory and combinatorial analysis, in: Proceedings of the fifth british combinatorial conference (univ. Aberdeen, Aberdeen, 1975), Congr. Numer.,15: 169–192, 1976. Moreover, a surprising connection between the eigenvalues of regular graphs $G$, [23] and this problem has been established by S. Brandt in [Br98].

(1): $n/2$ should be $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ in the statement. Otherwise five-cycle is a counterexample. (2): In the given reference, Keevash and Sudakov also proved it under the additional assumption that $G$ has $\geq \tfrac{n^2}{5}$ edges. ( The phrasing in their introduction suggests that this was the hard part, compared to proving it for $G$ with $\leq \tfrac{n^2}{12}$ edges. ) Later, Norin and Yepremyan (2015) proved $\geq (\tfrac{1}{5}- \gamma) n^2$ is enough, for some small but absolute $\gamma>0$. (3) Razborov (2021) proved it is true when $\tfrac{1}{50}$ is replaced with $\tfrac{27}{1024}$. According to Balogh, Cremen, Lidicky (2024), that is the most recent progress for this type of relaxation. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
Re (1): an alternative is to consider it as Razborov does in the reference you provided, which allows for a "2.5-vertex" subgraph. In that case, the five-cycle is not a counterexample.
To clarify, "subgraph" here means "induced subgraph" as per [Er93, p.344] [edit: typo: subgroup -> subgraph] (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
induced subgraph ;) (but indeed, $nK_1$ would otherwise be a counterex., so it is clear what the problem meant)
